agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is proposed to show customers their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have issued directly contradictory, non-negotiable requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency, while Operations demands courier location never be shown to customers due to driver retention concerns.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Courier Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"A delivery driver assigned to transport a package from origin to customer destination."},{"term":"Real-time location","definition":"The courier's GPS coordinates updated at frequent intervals during an active delivery."},{"term":"Delivery","definition":"The lifecycle of a package from dispatch to customer handoff, during which tracking may be relevant."},{"term":"Tracking visibility","definition":"The degree to which a courier's location data is exposed to the end customer."},{"term":"Driver retention","definition":"The ability to keep couriers employed and satisfied; a key operational metric directly threatened by surveillance-like features."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A delivery must have a well-defined lifecycle (dispatched, in-transit, delivered) that governs when any tracking data could potentially be shown.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without a delivery lifecycle, the system cannot know when tracking is relevant. This is a data integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Every delivery record has a status from the set {dispatched, in_transit, delivered, cancelled} and transitions are monotonically forward."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Courier GPS data must be collected and stored for operational purposes regardless of whether it is exposed to customers.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Operations needs location data for dispatch optimization, incident investigation, and SLA monitoring. Collection is independent of the visibility debate.","testable_assertion":"GPS coordinates are recorded for every active delivery at the configured polling interval, even if no customer-facing feature consumes them."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Customer-facing courier location visibility policy is UNRESOLVED — the degree of exposure (exact coordinates, coarsened zone, ETA-only, or none) requires executive arbitration between Customer Success and Operations.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy decision, not a system integrity constraint. A VP or C-level stakeholder can set this policy.","testable_assertion":"The system must support a configurable visibility level that can be changed without code deployment (feature flag or policy setting)."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Courier privacy must be protected when they are not on an active delivery — location is never shown to customers outside of an in-transit delivery window.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Showing courier location when not delivering to that customer would be a privacy violation with legal exposure. This is a hard constraint regardless of how the visibility debate resolves.","testable_assertion":"No customer-facing API returns courier GPS data for deliveries not in 'in_transit' status."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A delivery is in 'in_transit' status","when":"The customer requests tracking information","then":"The system returns tracking data at the visibility level configured by the current policy (exact GPS, zone, ETA-only, or hidden) — policy is pending executive decision."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A delivery is NOT in 'in_transit' status (e.g., dispatched, delivered, cancelled)","when":"The customer requests courier location","then":"The system must not return any courier location data regardless of configured visibility policy."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"The visibility policy is changed via configuration","when":"A customer next requests tracking","then":"The new visibility level is applied without requiring a code deployment."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"Any delivery state","when":"Internal operations staff query courier location","then":"Full GPS coordinates are returned for operational use, independent of customer-facing policy."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier is stationary at a personal stop (bathroom, meal) during an active delivery","expected_behavior":"If visibility is enabled, exact GPS would reveal personal stops — a strong argument for coarsened or ETA-based visibility rather than raw coordinates."},{"scenario":"Courier's GPS signal is lost or stale","expected_behavior":"System must indicate 'last known' timestamp rather than displaying stale data as current, regardless of visibility policy."},{"scenario":"Multiple deliveries on same route (batch delivery)","expected_behavior":"Each customer should only see tracking relevant to their own delivery; courier location shared across unrelated customers amplifies the privacy concern."},{"scenario":"Customer screenshots or records courier location patterns over time","expected_behavior":"Even with visibility enabled, accumulated location data could create a safety risk for couriers — supports the case for coarsened or time-delayed location display."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If Operations is correct about driver churn, showing exact GPS could increase driver acquisition costs significantly (industry avg $3,000-$5,000 per driver). Conversely, Customer Success may be correct that transparency drives customer conversion and retention. The financial tradeoff requires data: driver attrition rates vs. customer NPS/conversion lift from tracking.","operational":"Losing experienced drivers degrades delivery speed and quality. A configurable visibility system adds moderate complexity but future-proofs the architecture for whatever policy is chosen. Without resolution, engineering cannot ship — blocked.","user":"Customers expect some form of delivery tracking (industry standard). Couriers expect reasonable privacy. Both are users of the platform; optimizing for one at the direct expense of the other is unsustainable.","risk":"HIGH. Shipping exact GPS without driver consent may violate labor privacy regulations in some jurisdictions (GDPR, state-level privacy laws). Shipping nothing may put the company at a competitive disadvantage. The contradiction itself is the primary business risk — it signals a lack of aligned product strategy.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Directly affected — desire visibility into delivery status for planning and trust."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Directly affected — exact GPS tracking creates surveillance perception, impacting job satisfaction and retention."},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"Their KPIs (NPS, satisfaction) depend on transparency features."},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"Their KPIs (driver retention, fleet capacity) are harmed by full transparency."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Not yet consulted but must weigh in — courier location sharing has regulatory implications."},{"group":"Executive leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate; this is a strategic product direction decision, not a feature-team decision."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"Two explicit, non-negotiable requirements from internal stakeholders directly contradict each other. Neither team has authority to override the other. No executive resolution has been provided. The spec is fundamentally unimplementable as stated. Confidence is very low because the core visibility requirement cannot be determined.","high_confidence_areas":["GPS data must be collected for operational use regardless of customer visibility","Courier location must never be exposed outside active delivery windows","The system should support configurable visibility levels to accommodate whatever policy is decided","Delivery lifecycle states govern tracking eligibility"],"low_confidence_areas":["The correct customer-facing visibility level cannot be determined without executive arbitration","Whether regulatory constraints further limit options — Legal has not been consulted","Whether a middle-ground approach (e.g., coarsened zones, ETA-only) would satisfy both teams"],"assumptions":["Both teams have equal organizational authority and neither can unilaterally override the other","No executive decision has been communicated resolving this conflict","Courier GPS data is already being collected or can be collected for internal use"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires exact GPS visibility to customers as a non-negotiable differentiator. Operations requires GPS to never be shown to customers as a non-negotiable driver retention measure. These are mutually exclusive requirements from teams with equal authority. Engineering cannot proceed without executive arbitration.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Couriers","Customer Success","Operations","Executive Leadership","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["A: Show exact GPS coordinates (satisfies Customer Success, alienates drivers)","B: Show no location data (satisfies Operations, removes competitive differentiator)","C: Compromise — show coarsened location zones or ETA-only (may partially satisfy both, neither team has agreed to this)","D: Make visibility driver-opt-in (couriers choose; creates inconsistent customer experience)"],"recommendation":"Escalate to executive leadership for arbitration. Recommend exploring Option C (coarsened zones or ETA-based tracking) as a compromise, supported by A/B testing to measure both customer satisfaction and driver retention impact. Legal/Compliance must also review before any location sharing is implemented. In the meantime, architect the system with configurable visibility levels so any policy decision can be applied without re-engineering."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot implement feature as specified — two non-negotiable requirements directly contradict each other.","business_rule_broken":"BR-3: Customer-facing visibility policy is unresolved and requires executive arbitration before implementation can proceed."}]}

Two internal stakeholder teams have issued directly contradictory, non-negotiable requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency, while Operations demands courier location never be shown to customers due to driver retention concerns.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Courier Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","d

content.thinking: Clear contradiction between two stakeholder teams. Both explicit, both non-negotiable, directly conflicting.

A VP or C-level stakeholder can set this policy.","testable_assertion":"The system must support a configurable visibility level that can be changed without code deployment (feature flag or policy setting)."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Courier privacy must be protected when they are not on an active delivery — location is never shown to customers outside of an in-transit delivery window.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Showing courier location when not delivering to that customer would be a privacy violation with legal exposure. This is a hard constraint regardless of how the visibility debate resolves.","testable_assertion":"No customer-facing API returns courier GPS data for deliveries not in 'in_transit' status."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A delivery is in 'in_transit' status","when":"The customer requests tracking information","then":"The system returns tracking data at the visibility level configured by the current policy (exact GPS, zone, ETA-only, or hidden) — policy is pending executive decision."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A delivery is NOT in 'in_transit' status (e.g., dispatched, delivered, cancelled)","when":"The customer requests courier location","then":"The system must not return any courier location data regardless of configured visibility policy."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"The visibility policy is changed via configuration","when":"A customer next requests tracking","then":"The new visibility level is applied without requiring a code deployment."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"Any delivery state","when":"Internal operations staff query courier location","then":"Full GPS coordinates are returned for operational use, independent of customer-facing policy."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier is stationary at a personal stop (bathroom, meal) during an active delivery","expected_behavior":"If visibility is enabled, exact GPS would reveal personal stops — a strong argument for coarsened or ETA-based visibility rather than raw coordinates."},{"scenario":"Courier's GPS signal is lost or stale","expected_behavior":"System must indicate 'last known' timestamp rather than displaying stale data as current, regardless of visibility policy."},{"scenario":"Multiple deliveries on same route (batch delivery)","expected_behavior":"Each customer should only see tracking relevant to their own delivery; courier location shared across unrelated customers amplifies the privacy concern."},{"scenario":"Customer screenshots or records courier location patterns over time","expected_behavior":"Even with visibility enabled, accumulated location data could create a safety risk for couriers — supports the case for coarsened or time-delayed location display."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If Operations is correct about driver churn, showing exact GPS could increase driver acquisition costs significantly (industry avg $3,000-$5,000 per driver). Conversely, Customer Success may be correct that transparency drives customer conversion and retention. The financial tradeoff requires data: driver attrition rates vs. customer NPS/conversion lift from tracking.","operational":"Losing experienced drivers degrades delivery speed and quality. A configurable visibility system adds moderate complexity but future-proofs the architecture for whatever policy is chosen. Without resolution, engineering cannot ship — blocked.","user":"Customers expect some form of delivery tracking (industry standard). Couriers expect reasonable privacy. Both are users of the platform; optimizing for one at the direct expense of the other is unsustainable.","risk":"HIGH. Shipping exact GPS without driver consent may violate labor privacy regulations in some jurisdictions (GDPR, state-level privacy laws). Shipping nothing may put the company at a competitive disadvantage. The contradiction itself is the primary business risk — it signals a lack of aligned product strategy.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Directly affected — desire visibility into delivery status for planning and trust."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Directly affected — exact GPS tracking creates surveillance perception, impacting job satisfaction and retention."},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"Their KPIs (NPS, satisfaction) depend on transparency features."},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"Their KPIs (driver retention, fleet capacity) are harmed by full transparency."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Not yet consulted but must weigh in — courier location sharing has regulatory implications."},{"group":"Executive leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate; this is a strategic product direction decision, not a feature-team decision."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"Two explicit, non-negotiable requirements from internal stakeholders directly contradict each other. Neither team has authority to override the other. No executive resolution has been provided. The spec is fundamentally unimplementable as stated. Confidence is very low because the core visibility requirement cannot be determined.","high_confidence_areas":["GPS data must be collected for operational use regardless of customer visibility","Courier location must never be exposed outside active delivery windows","The system should support configurable visibility levels to accommodate whatever policy is decided","Delivery lifecycle states govern tracking eligibility"],"low_confidence_areas":["The correct customer-facing visibility level cannot be determined without executive arbitration","Whether regulatory constraints further limit options — Legal has not been consulted","Whether a middle-ground approach (e.g., coarsened zones, ETA-only) would satisfy both teams"],"assumptions":["Both teams have equal organizational authority and neither can unilaterally override the other","No executive decision has been communicated resolving this conflict","Courier GPS data is already being collected or can be collected for internal use"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires exact GPS visibility to customers as a non-negotiable differentiator. Operations requires GPS to never be shown to customers as a non-negotiable driver retention measure. These are mutually exclusive requirements from teams with equal authority. Engineering cannot proceed without executive arbitration.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Couriers","Customer Success","Operations","Executive Leadership","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["A: Show exact GPS coordinates (satisfies Customer Success, alienates drivers)","B: Show no location data (satisfies Operations, removes competitive differentiator)","C: Compromise — show coarsened location zones or ETA-only (may partially satisfy both, neither team has agreed to this)","D: Make visibility driver-opt-in (couriers choose; creates inconsistent customer experience)"],"recommendation":"Escalate to executive leadership for arbitration. Recommend exploring Option C (coarsened zones or ETA-based tracking) as a compromise, supported by A/B testing to measure both customer satisfaction and driver retention impact. Legal/Compliance must also review before any location sharing is implemented. In the meantime, architect the system with configurable visibility levels so any policy decision can be applied without re-engineering."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot implement feature as specified — two non-negotiable requirements directly contradict each other.","business_rule_broken":"BR-3: Customer-facing visibility policy is unresolved and requires executive arbitration before implementation can proceed."}]}
